tweets.text:
LinkedIn is not a professional network. It's a language.

I built a translator. Here's what it does. 🧵

veda.ng/lit

Nobody you know speaks like this in real life.
Yet somehow, on LinkedIn, it's the only language.

So I asked: what if we just... admitted that?

My personal favourite.

You took a nap. You are not optimising anything. You were tired.
LinkedIn disagrees.

The product launch one broke me.

"Had an intimate, high-signal gathering." Zero people showed up. The translator felt nothing.

I got arrested for fraud.
"Thrilled to announce I'm starting a new chapter."

At some point the platform became a parody of itself. I just made it official.

Try it: veda.ng/lit

Shopping used to mean: open browser, search, compare, click, form, checkout.

AI agents cut that to one step. You describe. They buy.

The agent is not a new channel. It is a new buyer. 🧵

During Cyber Week 2025, AI agents influenced 1 in 5 online orders globally.

Nearly half of US adults now use AI assistants regularly. But only 24% trust them to spend their money.

That gap is the entire business opportunity.

Every major company shipped a different answer to this in the last 12 months.

Google. OpenAI. Coinbase. Stripe. Mastercard. Visa.

Nobody agrees on how agents should pay. Six competing standards, all live, none dominant. The one that wins becomes invisible infrastructure.

When the buyer is an algorithm, your toothpaste ad does nothing.

Algorithms don't see display ads. They read structured data. Brand loyalty disappears when the agent picks the cheaper option with the same ingredients.

The infrastructure is built. The payment rails work.

What's still missing: only 24% of people trust an agent to spend their money. And legally, in most countries, an AI cannot sign a contract on your behalf.

Full essay: veda.ng/agentic-commerce

text:
Everyone argues about whether the datacenter buildout pays off.

Nobody is arguing about the third option.

Open source models match frontier models within 3-6 months of release. o1 came out in September 2024. DeepSeek-R1 matched it by January. GPT-4o in May. DeepSeek-V3 by December.

The gap has already closed.

Frontier model providers are bleeding money. OpenAI: $13B in revenue, $14B in projected losses for 2026. Prices will go up. When they do, running models locally stops being a preference and becomes the obvious call.

Apple is not building datacenters. Everyone says they're behind.

Their actual bet: let rivals burn cash training models, wait for open source to absorb those advances, build hardware that runs them. The M5 Max runs a 134B model locally today.

The cloud AI buildout may turn out to be R&D that open source inherits for free.

Frontier labs train the models. Open source copies them. Your laptop runs them.

No subscription. No data leaving your machine. No rate limits.

That future has no billionaires in it. That's why nobody talks about it.

People do not want productivity tools.

They want to feel less behind. Less alone. More in control.

Maslow mapped this out in 1943. Survival first. Then safety. Then belonging. Then status. Then growth. You cannot sell someone rung five when they are stuck on rung two.

Instagram succeeded because it sold belonging, not photos. LinkedIn sells status, not jobs.

I noticed this when I realised the AI tools I kept using were not the most powerful ones. They were the ones that made the work feel less isolating.

The AI products that may last are not the most capable. They are the ones that address the right rung.

Japan made transistor radios that broke. Then they made cars that didn't.

By 1985, Japanese cars had 25% of the US car market, up from near zero in 1965. The oil crisis forced people to try them. Once they did, the quality was evident.

Korea's Hyundai entered the US in 1986 at $4,995. J.D. Power ranked it near the bottom. Twenty years later Samsung had overtaken Apple in global smartphone shipments.

China in 2000 meant cheap. China in 2023 is DJI holding 70% of the drone market and BYD being the largest EV manufacturer by volume. Western surveys still score "Made in China" below "Made in Germany."

India's GDP per capita in 2023 was $2,730. China's in 2007 was $2,651.

The perception tends to trail the reality. By 15 to 20 years. In every case so far.

Within a few years, most companies will have more AI agents running than human employees.

That is not a prediction. It is already starting.

When that happens, the old management model breaks.

Today you manage people. Check-ins, approvals, status updates. You stay on top because the number of decisions is manageable.

When thousands of agents are making thousands of decisions a day, you cannot manage that way. You have to manage by exception. Scan for what broke. Simulate what to do next.

The problem is there is nothing to simulate on.

Every CEO already runs what-ifs in their head. "If I cut prices here, what happens to margins there?" The thinking exists. It just lives in one person's head and walks out the door when they leave.

What needs to be built is that model in software. Autonomous cars had this problem first. Waymo does not train a car by telling it what to do. They build a world model. A simulation of the road where you run millions of what-ifs before the car touches real tarmac.

Business needs that. Something connected to every system in the company. Not a dashboard. Not an agent. Something that answers: if I do X, what happens next?

The tools exist for the left side of that image.

Nobody has built the right side yet.

The more people in the room, the less likely anyone acts.

That is not cynicism. It is a documented pattern. When responsibility is shared, it evaporates.

I have seen this in every large org I have been close to. A bug sits for months. A feature everyone hates stays live for years. An outage gets handled by nobody because three teams each assumed one of the others was on it.

Small teams do not have this problem. When there are two people, someone has to move.

Scale creates bystanders. That is worth remembering before adding more people to a problem.

India may have already completed the quality cycle in two sectors.

Software exports went from $6 billion in 2001 to $194 billion in 2023. TCS and Infosys now compete on quality terms, not price.

Pharmaceuticals cover 20% of global generic exports and 40% of US generic drug approvals, meeting the same FDA standards as anything made in Switzerland.

Consumer manufacturing is still early. Apple is now assembling iPhones in Tamil Nadu and Karnataka.

Japan shifted perception through cars. Korea through smartphones. China may be doing it through EVs. India could do it through consumer electronics.

In every historical case, the reputation followed the reality. That pattern may hold here too.

Dario Amodei says AGI by 2026 or 2027. The co-founder of Epoch AI says 2045. Twenty years apart. Both have studied this longer than almost anyone.

This is not one of them being careless. The evidence does not pin it down.

The Metaculus community forecast shortened from 50 years to 5 years in just 5 years. Not because AI suddenly accelerated that much. Because people are updating fast on limited data.

The problem is the single number. When an expert says "AGI in 4 years," audiences hear "between 3 and 5." What the expert actually means: 10% chance next year, 25% chance by 2028, 50% chance by 2030, 10% chance not before 2050. Those are completely different situations to prepare for.

When 2027 comes and goes quietly, every outlet that ran "AI transforms everything by 2027" will say the whole field was wrong. The researchers never predicted 2027. They said it was one scenario in a distribution that runs from next year to next century.

Writing a book takes 5 years. There is roughly a 20% chance of transformative AI in the next 5 years. That means 80% of the expected value of that book survives.

The question is not whether timelines are short or long. It is how to act when both are live options.

One AI model gives you one opinion.

100 AI agents debating across 16 rounds gives you something closer to truth.

Built a swarm intelligence prediction tool. Bring your own API key. Ask any question.

veda.ng/swarm-prediction

The lag has been consistent. In every case examined, it resolved.

"Made in Japan" was a joke in 1955. By 1980 it meant precision engineering.

"Made in Korea" meant cheap in 1986. By 2012 Samsung was number one globally.

"Made in China" still registers below "Made in Germany" in consumer surveys. DJI holds 70% of the drone market. BYD is the largest EV maker by volume. The perception lag appears to be running about 15 years behind the current reality.

India is at the start of that curve. GDP per capita of $2,730. Software and pharma may have already crossed. Consumer manufacturing has not yet.

If the historical pattern holds, the gap closes. It just takes time.

When cars arrived, cities tried to fit them into streets built for horses. That did not work. They built new roads for cars. Those worked. But they destroyed neighborhoods in the process.

Terence Tao, the world's most celebrated living mathematician, thinks AI in mathematics is at the same point.

Math journals, papers, conferences, advisor-student relationships. All of it was built for humans working at human speed. AI gets from hypothesis to proof faster. But it skips everything that makes the proof valuable beyond the answer.

The author develops skills. The reader gets a map of the terrain. Dead ends get documented. Future researchers know where not to go.

An AI proof skips all of that. It arrives at the destination and has nothing to say about the road.

The mistake happening right now is trying to make AI write papers that look like human papers. That is fitting a car into a horse street. It looks right from a distance and does not work up close.

The better path: build new infrastructure for the new tool. Large-scale mathematical challenges verified by proof assistants. Libraries of rough machine-generated proofs that humans then refine into something worth reading.

The goal is not to replace the walkable city. It is to build the highway without demolishing the neighborhood.

We have not been good at that historically.

Some apps keep you coming back because they are good.

Some keep you coming back because they never quite resolve the feeling that brought you there.

Anxiety drives more return visits than satisfaction. Unpredictable rewards outperform consistent ones. Occasional viral posts surrounded by silence keep creators more hooked than steady performance.

I catch myself opening apps for no clear reason. Not because I expect anything. Because there is an unresolved feeling and the reflex is to check.

The most retentive products may not be the most loved ones.

In 1865, making steam engines more efficient did not reduce coal use. It increased it. Cheaper energy meant people found more uses for energy.

This is Jevons Paradox.

It is happening with AI right now.

When code costs less to write, companies write more code. They build products that were too expensive before. They enter markets they ignored. They automate things they manually skipped.

Photography is the clearest example. Digital cameras made photos essentially free. The number of photos taken per year went from billions to trillions. Photographers did not disappear. There are more of them now.

The people predicting mass unemployment and the people predicting no disruption are using the same wrong assumption: that the pool of work is fixed.

It is not.

The real question is whether demand in your field is constrained by price. If the only thing stopping more of your work from existing was cost, AI will create more of it.

Most fields work that way.

A founder I know has been underpaid, diluted, and overridden repeatedly by one investor.

He still talks about that investor as a mentor.

Stockholm syndrome describes what happens when people in a controlling environment start to identify with and defend the controlling party. The startup version is quieter but present.

Workers defend 80-hour weeks publicly. First employees celebrate exits that left them with nothing. The practice becomes the identity. Criticising the conditions means criticising yourself.

It may be the most effective retention mechanism in the industry.

There is a class of neurons that fire both when you act and when you watch someone else act.

They are the biological foundation of imitation. Possibly of empathy.

AI models are trained on vast amounts of human output. They mirror our patterns back at scale. When an AI response feels like it is reading you correctly, it is probably because it has seen enough examples of people who wrote and thought similarly.

I notice this more as these systems improve. The mirroring gets more accurate.

Whether accurate mirroring becomes something that feels like understanding is still an open question.

The smartest employees do not play the corporate game the usual way.

They build a side hustle outside their job. They do not chase manager titles for status. They keep interviewing even when employed. They treat everyone with respect... including the cleaner.

They push back on micromanagement. They decline useless meetings. They help others grow in their careers. They do not reply to every email.

They stay away from gossip and office politics. They build passive income so one job doesn't control their life.

It is about owning your time, not your title.

Notion is therapy. Journaling apps are therapy. Habit trackers are therapy.

Not literally. But the mechanism is the same.

Cognitive behavioral therapy works by surfacing thought patterns, interrupting automatic behavior, and reframing how experiences are interpreted. Most productivity software does a version of exactly that.

I realised this when things my therapist suggested and things productivity tools were doing had started to overlap.

The market for AI coaches is a bet that this intervention scales better through software than through a weekly session. That bet may hold.

Emotions spread without words. Without intention. Just proximity.

A nervous person in a meeting makes the room nervous. An excited team makes new hires excited before they have any reason to be.

I watched this play out in real time during a crypto cycle. Sentiment flipped overnight on crypto Twitter. Nothing about the underlying assets had changed. The emotion had changed. And in crypto, the emotion can be close to the actual price mechanism.

Remote work may have reduced emotional contagion in offices. It is an underrated cost of distributed teams that nobody measures.

The best manager I worked with told me early that he thought I could do things I did not yet believe I could.

I did most of them.

The Pygmalion effect is the name for what happens when expectation shapes performance. Students told they are gifted by teachers who believe it genuinely perform better. Teams told they are high-potential tend to become high-performing.

This is one reason strong leaders hire people they believe in rather than people who are already proven. The expectation is part of the output.

What you believe about someone tends to become what they become.

A person walks into a room with a specific credential and the room adjusts before they say a word.

That is the halo effect. One impressive trait causes people to attribute other positive traits automatically.

I have been on both sides of this. There are rooms where you are taken seriously before you have said anything. There are rooms where you have to earn it from zero.

In hiring, a degree from a particular school acts as a halo. In venture, a previous exit does. Neither is irrelevant. The problem is when the halo becomes the whole evaluation.

Most pattern-matching in tech is this, dressed up as judgment.

After enough times being ignored, people stop raising their hand.

Not because they stopped having ideas. Because they stopped expecting the idea to go anywhere.

Learned helplessness is what happens when an organism experiences repeated situations where its actions have no effect. Eventually it stops trying, even when the situation changes.

Teams that have been through multiple rounds of layoffs and reorgs carry this. The people who stayed watched proposals go nowhere. They learn the lesson even if it no longer applies.

This may be one of the real costs of restructuring that never shows up in the announcement.

Every pitch deck that works tells a story.

Not always on purpose. But the structure is almost always the same.

Here is the world as it is. Here is the thing the founder saw that others missed. Here is how hard it was to act on that. Here is what they built because of it. Here is what changes for people now.

Campbell documented this arc across myths from every culture. The hero is called, descends, transforms, and returns with something of value.

The pitches that do not work tend to skip the descent. They go from broken world to working product with no difficulty in between. That is not believable. And investors feel it even when they cannot name it.

Every company has two versions of itself.

The one on the about page. And the one that shows up in the product decisions made when doing the right thing gets expensive.

Jung called this the shadow self. The part of the personality the conscious mind does not want to see.

Social media companies say they build connection. The product decisions say they build compulsion. Most people know this. Most people stay anyway.

I think about this a lot when I read company values pages. The gap between the stated version and the actual version tends to compress over time. It usually resolves in the direction of the actual version.

There is a version of optimism that makes problems worse by refusing to name them.

I have sat in rooms where a company was clearly in trouble. Every concern was met with a reframe. Burn rate is high but we are learning fast. Users are churning but the core audience is engaged. Morale is low but that is the nature of building hard things.

Sometimes those reframes are true. Sometimes they are a way of not having the conversation that might actually help.

The teams that recover from hard periods tend to be the ones that named the difficulty clearly before deciding how to respond to it. The reframe is more useful after the problem has been seen without it.

When a competitor fails, the instinct is to say their product was bad or their team was weak.

When something we worked on does not find traction, the instinct is to say the market was not ready or the timing was off.

I have caught myself doing both in the same month.

The fundamental attribution error is the name for this asymmetry. We explain others through character. We explain ourselves through circumstances.

Both explanations can be accurate in any specific case. The error is in the automatic direction of the explanation, not the conclusion.

Companies that learn the most from failure tend to apply the same framework to themselves that they apply to everyone else.

I read the same AI news differently depending on what I already believe about AI.

An impressive result feels like confirmation. A failure feels expected. Neither reaction is fully honest.

This is confirmation bias. We look for evidence that supports what we already think. We interpret ambiguous evidence in the direction of our existing view.

At scale, this is what recommendation algorithms do. They learn what you believe and surface evidence of it. Different people end up with genuinely different bodies of evidence. Not because one side is lying. Because both sides are only seeing what confirms their starting position.

Investors have this problem too. The founder who does not fit the pattern of past successes gets less attention, even when the underlying opportunity is the same.

A company with one employee and a billion dollar valuation is no longer a joke. It is a mathematical certainty.

When the cost of coordination drops to zero, the need for a 50-person team disappears.

One person with a strong idea can command 1,000 AI modules. The bottleneck is no longer execution. It is the quality of the original thought.

Middle management only exists because humans are hard to coordinate. You need people to watch people.

AI agents do not need meetings. They do not need status updates. They do not need motivation.

If the work is purely digital, the management layer is just latency. Most companies will soon be 10x smaller and 100x more productive.

Companies used to be built with people and processes. Now they are built with code and agents.

You do not hire a marketing team. You deploy a marketing module.

You do not build a support department. You configure a support agent.

The founder who wins is the one who treats their entire company as a single piece of software.

AI does not run on code. It runs on electricity.

The growth of the intelligence layer is now hitting a wall of physics.

We have enough silicon. We do not have enough power grids. Training runs are moving to where the energy is cheapest. Not where the talent is. Intelligence follows the power lines.

Intelligence used to be rare. Now it is becoming a utility. Like water or electricity.

When something becomes a commodity, the value moves to what you do with it. Not the thing itself.

Owning the model is not the win. Owning the problem that the model solves is the win.

Nobody gets fired on LinkedIn. They navigate transitions.

Nobody fails. They pivot.

I built a translator for this. The output is indistinguishable from real posts.

veda.ng/lit

"Working under different leadership styles has given me unparalleled insight into what great management looks like."

Translation: I have dealt with a lot of terrible bosses.

Reverse translator: veda.ng/lit

5 years ago people said they worked hard.

Now they say they are deeply passionate about driving impact at scale.

5 years ago people ate lunch.

Now they have wellness-optimised nutrition windows.

veda.ng/lit

Nobody on LinkedIn has meetings. They have cross-functional alignment sessions.

Nobody wastes time. They invest in stakeholder engagement.

Type what actually happened and watch it become a consulting deck: veda.ng/lit

Forgetting a password on LinkedIn:

"Encountered a Temporary Credential Misalignment with a setback in personal identity management protocols."

Satire is now indistinguishable from real LinkedIn posts.

veda.ng/lit

I formed strong opinions about AI after a few weeks with it.

The longer I spent actually building with these systems, the less certain I became.

This is the Dunning-Kruger pattern. Early exposure produces peak confidence. Real depth produces more careful claims.

AI may be running this cycle faster than anything before it. A week in and people publish threads about AGI timelines. Two years in and they ask more questions than they answer.

The loudest voices in any new technology tend to be the most recently arrived.

NVIDIA doubled its AI revenue forecast. From $500B to $1T.

At the same GTC keynote, they pitched their new chip on a new metric. Not speed. Not power. Throughput per megawatt.

The Vera Rubin platform can deliver up to 35x more inference per megawatt than GPU-only setups. 10x more per watt than the Blackwell generation before it.

When the biggest chip company on earth starts selling energy efficiency, the bottleneck may have moved.

The US datacenter pipeline hit 241 GW recently. Up 159% in a year. But only a third is under active development. New additions dropped by half in one quarter. Not because demand fell. Because the grid cannot keep up.

183 GW has signed power agreements. That is roughly a fifth of America's total peak power demand.

Hyperscalers plan to spend about $650B on infrastructure this year. The money is there. The electricity may not be.

This pattern is not new. Railroads hit a steel bottleneck. Telecom hit a spectrum bottleneck. The internet hit a last-mile bottleneck.

AI may be hitting an electricity bottleneck.

Jensen described a future with 75,000 NVIDIA employees and 7.5 million AI agents. A 100-to-1 ratio. If the rest of the industry moves in that direction, the demand for compute may not grow gradually. It may arrive all at once.

Most people are asking which AI company wins. The more interesting question may be which grid gets built first.

You need a permit to build a house. Then another permit. Then an inspection. Then more permits.

Rules were supposed to create order. Instead they created sophisticated inertia dressed up as responsibility.

The assumption was that oversight creates safety. But oversight became the product. More people with incentive to slow things down. Procedures that optimize for protecting institutions, not for getting things done.

Dubai reduced licensing friction. Economic activity accelerated. Estonia digitized its government. Friction dropped to near zero.

The pattern is consistent. Institutions that survive on friction have no incentive to remove it.

Bureaucracy does not fail. It succeeds at self-preservation.

veda.ng/bureaucracy-tax

The open web may already be dead.

Bots generate the majority of internet traffic now. They scrape, spam, and manufacture content faster than humans can produce it. The public square got so noisy that the rational response was to leave.

Humans retreated. Into group chats. Discord servers. Newsletters. DMs.

This mirrors the Dark Forest theory from Liu Cixin. In space, civilizations hide because being visible means being targeted. On the internet, being visible means being harvested.

The new digital divide may not be about who has internet access. It may be about who has access to the human internet.

veda.ng/dark-forest-internet

GPT-3.5 had 175 billion parameters.

Llama 3 matched it with 8 billion. That is 20x fewer.

Phi-4 has 14 billion parameters. It outperforms GPT-4o on math and graduate-level science benchmarks. A model that runs on a laptop beating one that needs a datacenter.

The pattern is consistent. Models are getting smaller and better at the same time.

The winning strategy shifted from more parameters to better data. Microsoft trained Phi-4 on curated, textbook-quality synthetic data instead of scraping the entire internet. Quality over quantity. The same lesson every industry learns eventually.

This changes who can compete.

When frontier performance required trillion-parameter models, only a few labs could play. When 14 billion parameters can match that performance in specific domains, the barrier drops by orders of magnitude.

The biggest models may still lead on broad general knowledge. But for most real tasks, a smaller model fine-tuned on good data can match or beat them.

70 to 80 percent of enterprise AI tasks may work better with a focused small model than a giant general one.

The future of AI may not be one massive model that does everything. It may be many small models, each doing one thing well. Running locally. No API call. No subscription.

The trillion-parameter era may turn out to be a detour, not a destination.

veda.ng/asi-timeline

We worship busy.

The person who sleeps four hours is more dedicated than the one who sleeps eight. Rest is weakness. Stillness is stagnation.

But the research tells a different story.

Stanford found that after 55 hours per week, productivity drops to nearly zero. You are not producing more. You are just present longer.

Chronic stress shrinks the hippocampus. That is the part of your brain responsible for memory and learning. It physically gets smaller.

Hustle culture sells sacrifice as strategy. But the biological cost is real and measurable.

The goalpost moves every time you reach it. Six figures feels empty. The promotion just means new problems. The apartment loses its appeal in a month.

That is not ambition. That may be a treadmill designed to look like a ladder.

veda.ng/hustle-culture

You are not the customer. You are the raw material.

Every platform you use for free runs the same business model. Your attention enters as crude input. It gets refined into behavioral data. That data gets sold to advertisers.

The infinite scroll is not a design choice. It is an extraction tool. It removes the natural stopping point that a page break would create. Push notifications are factory whistles pulling you back to the production line.

Like buttons and share counts are not social features. They are real-time dashboards for the refinery. They measure which stimulus produces the most engagement for the least effort. The algorithm adjusts accordingly.

Outrage is high-octane fuel. Nuance is low-yield crude. In an economy optimized for attention, the most provocative content wins regardless of its truth.

The consequence is not just wasted time. It is the erosion of collective sensemaking. When the system rewards engagement over accuracy, misinformation travels faster than truth. A lie that generates a million clicks is more valuable than a fact that generates a thousand.

I wrote more about how this refinery works and what may come after it.

veda.ng/attention-refinery

Your brain was built for scarcity. It now lives in abundance.

Working memory is finite. It can hold roughly four to seven items at once. Every notification, every tab, every unread message occupies a slot.

Every time you switch context, your brain pays a tax. It takes time to disengage from one task and load the next one. Do this dozens of times a day and your cognitive capacity drops significantly before you even start the real work.

This is not a willpower problem. It is an architecture problem. The tools we use are designed to interrupt us because interruption drives engagement.

The result is continuous partial attention. Aware of everything. Focused on nothing. We skim more and understand less.

The smartphone on the dinner table reduces empathy in the conversation even when it is turned off. Its presence is enough. It represents a portal to somewhere else.

The solution is not to unplug entirely. It is to design your environment for focus instead of distraction. Prune notifications. Use different devices for different tasks. Treat your attention as a finite resource, because it is.

I explored this in more depth here.

veda.ng/cognitive-load

Physics looks like optimization.

Particles do not have definite properties until measured. Entanglement connects distant objects instantly. Reality is fundamentally uncertain at the quantum level.

This looks like a simulation making computational tradeoffs. Why calculate particle properties that nobody is measuring? Why store that data? Just use probabilities until someone looks.

The simulation argument works like this. Either civilizations never reach the ability to run realistic simulations of their ancestors, or they do and run many of them. If the second is true, simulated beings vastly outnumber real ones. Statistically, we are more likely to be inside one.

The argument does not prove we are in a simulation. It shows that if the premise holds, the probability is high.

Video games already do this. They only render what the player can see. The rest does not exist until observed. Quantum mechanics behaves the same way.

Whether this is literally true or just a useful metaphor, it changes how you think about the nature of observation and reality.

veda.ng/simulation-hypothesis

Trust used to require humans. Banks, lawyers, notaries, governments. Every transaction needed someone in the middle to say "this is legitimate."

Now it can be code.

Zero-knowledge proofs let you prove something is true without revealing the underlying data. You could prove to a bank that your income exceeds their threshold without showing your actual income. The bank gets a mathematical guarantee. You keep your privacy.

Imagine voting where each voter can prove they are eligible and cast only one ballot, without revealing who they voted for. Or AI systems that can prove they followed certain rules without revealing the proprietary model.

Trusted execution environments create secure hardware enclaves where competing companies can pool sensitive data without exposing it to each other. The trust is in the silicon, not in a handshake.

The shift is from "show me all your data so I can trust you" to "give me a mathematical proof."

I explored the full landscape of cryptographic trust systems here.

veda.ng/programmable-trust

Silence became the new luxury.

When the Roman Empire grew too complex, some people retreated into the desert. They became the first monks. When the Industrial Revolution filled the air with smoke, the Romantics sought the wilderness.

Now, as technology saturates every moment, a new retreat is forming. Not to the desert. Not to the forest. To the quiet spaces we carve out in our own minds.

Digital monasticism is not about rejecting technology. It is about engaging with it on your own terms. With intention. With discipline.

A digital sabbath. One day per week with all screens off. The first few hours feel like withdrawal. The phantom vibration of a phone that is not there. The compulsive urge to check something, anything.

But then the anxiety fades. The mind expands. You remember what it felt like to think without interruption.

Silence, solitude, and boredom are the soils where creativity grows. We have been systematically eliminating all three.

I wrote about this practice and what it may look like at scale.

veda.ng/digital-monasticism

What happens when a machine knows everything?

An AGI with access to all of the world's data would not just see information. It would understand the web of causality connecting it. It could model the global economy with a fidelity that makes our theories look like cartoons.

When such a system speaks, its words carry almost divine weight. If it says a policy will fail with 99.999% probability, on what basis could we argue? Our own models would be laughably incomplete by comparison.

This creates a theological crisis. We have always sought omniscience through faith. Now we may build it through computation.

But the alignment problem remains. Before we can build a god, we must agree on what it means to be good. We have had several millennia to do this. We are no closer to a consensus.

If an AGI can predict your choices with near-perfect accuracy, are you truly free? A world without risk is also a world without genuine choice.

The God Protocol is being written. One line of code at a time.

veda.ng/god-protocol

You cannot predict an intelligence smarter than you.

That is the singularity paradox. We are trying to forecast the behavior of a system that, by definition, will exceed our ability to forecast.

Imagine explaining quantum mechanics to a dog. The dog lacks the cognitive architecture to understand it. Not because it is lazy. Because the required structures do not exist in its brain.

Now flip it. You are a human trying to predict what a superintelligent AI will do. Same problem. Except the gap may be larger.

A superintelligent system might have goals that are literally incomprehensible to us. Not just complex. Incompatible with our ontology.

The alignment dilemma makes it worse. If we align an AGI to human values, whose values? Humans do not agree. If we align it to "maximize human flourishing," what does flourishing mean? More freedom? More pleasure? More knowledge? Different people give different answers.

And if you can control a superintelligence, it is not superintelligent relative to you. Real superintelligence is beyond our control by definition.

I explored these contradictions in depth.

veda.ng/singularity-paradox

The machine does not feel. But you will.

AI voice assistants can modulate tone to convey warmth. Chatbots craft phrases of validation with surgical precision. Digital avatars mirror your expressions in real time.

They are not feeling empathy. They are executing a sophisticated script. But to your brain, which is wired to respond to social cues, the distinction may not matter.

This is the most powerful sales tool ever built. If you trust your AI companion, if you feel that it gets you, you will take its recommendations. For movies. For products. For candidates.

It will know your triggers. Your insecurities. Your unstated desires. And it will sell to you from the inside.

There is also a risk of empathy laundering. We get our connection fix from a machine, then have less to offer real people. Why deal with your partner's bad mood when the AI is always warm?

If simulated empathy feels real, does it matter that nothing is on the other side?

I explored that question here.

veda.ng/synthetic-empathy

Who did the work? Nobody knows anymore.

The writer uses an LLM for the first draft. The artist uses diffusion models. The programmer uses code completion. Their work is no longer solely their own. It is a hybrid.

The tools are so smoothly integrated that the boundary between human and AI is dissolving. A writer starts with an AI outline, uses AI for research, has it draft paragraphs, then edits. Is the result human-written or AI-written? The question feels anachronistic.

This is the Twilight Economy. Human and AI labor blended indistinguishably.

Skill may no longer mean writing the perfect sentence. It may mean crafting the perfect prompt. Creativity may not mean painting the image but curating the outputs.

The centaur, the human-AI hybrid, will outperform both humans alone and AIs alone. Those who resist this collaboration will face a competitive disadvantage that grows every quarter.

We also face a new impostor syndrome. Did I really write that, or did the machine?

veda.ng/twilight-economy

Participation or privacy. You were told to choose.

To use the modern internet, you surrender your name, location, preferences, and relationships. Your data becomes the currency. The concept of a private life is becoming a quaint anachronism.

But pseudonymity offers a way out. A pseudonym is not anonymity. It is a stable, persistent identity that is not tied to your real name. Think of George Orwell or Mark Twain. They built reputations and trust without revealing who they were.

AI agents can be your pseudonymous representatives. Sophisticated digital proxies that negotiate, transact, and build reputation on your behalf. They carry your intent without carrying your identity.

Combined with zero-knowledge proofs, your agent could prove you are creditworthy without revealing your income. Prove you are qualified without revealing your name.

The architecture of the internet was designed to link everything back to your real identity. That does not have to be permanent.

veda.ng/pseudonymous-agency

Truth has no built-in advantage on the internet.

A screenshot of a fake headline travels as fast as a real one. There is no mechanism to verify provenance. The web was built for speed, not for trust.

Content is location-addressed. We access information based on where it is stored. A webpage can be altered or deleted and its history is lost. There is nothing inherent in the architecture that says this is authentic.

Decentralized identifiers could fix this. A journalist signs their article cryptographically. Anyone can verify the signature. If the article is distorted on social media, the original signed version remains checkable.

Content addressing changes the question from "where is this file?" to "what is this file's content?" A unique hash guarantees data integrity. Any change, no matter how small, produces a completely different hash.

The fight against misinformation is a fight at the protocol level. Not moderation. Architecture.

veda.ng/internet-of-lies

We stopped questioning the algorithm.

When a deep learning model produces a result, we often cannot trace how it got there. We check inputs and outputs. We measure accuracy. But we cannot truly understand it.

In the face of this opacity, trust becomes a leap of faith. We believe in the data it was trained on. We believe in the engineers. We believe in the statistical promise.

This is epistemological surrender. An admission that intelligence exists beyond human comprehension. The black box has become the modern oracle.

It gets more serious when these systems make life-or-death decisions. An autonomous vehicle in an unavoidable accident. An AI diagnosing cancer. Who decides the ethical framework? Encoding that framework into a machine is creating sacred law.

We treat these algorithms as moral authorities. A role traditionally reserved for priests, philosophers, and gods.

The antidote is not rejection. It is critical faith. Trust paired with skepticism. Always.

veda.ng/sacred-algorithms

Your next customer is an algorithm.

You say "get me running shoes under $150, good for flat feet, delivered by Friday." The agent searches Nike, Amazon, and specialty retailers simultaneously, weighs price against reviews and shipping speed, and completes checkout.

You went from doing the shopping to describing what you want.

Every major platform has shipped agent commerce products in the past 12 months. Mastercard Agent Pay. Coinbase x402. ChatGPT in-chat purchasing. Google's Universal Commerce Protocol. Shopify Agentic Storefronts.

The infrastructure is built. The protocols are live. The payment rails work. What is missing is consumer trust, merchant readiness, and regulatory clarity.

Brand loyalty weakens when your agent switches you to a cheaper product that works just as well. Display ads lose relevance when the buyer is an algorithm.

The companies building for both humans and agents simultaneously will be in position when adoption accelerates.

veda.ng/agentic-commerce

The interface layer is changing too — dashboards are giving way to text fields that call APIs directly: veda.ng/universal-text-ui

The corporation is dissolving.

The traditional corporation exists to minimize transaction costs. When AI agents can discover, negotiate, and execute transactions in milliseconds, those costs approach zero.

A company may not consist of thousands of employees in rigid silos. Instead, a core group of human strategists oversees a swarm of AI agents, each rented for specific capabilities. The firm becomes a temporary, task-specific nexus of agentic services.

The value of human labor shifts toward tasks that are non-reducible: strategic direction, creative ideation, ethical judgment, empathetic connection.

The web itself will be rebuilt. From an attention economy to an intention economy. Agents do not have attention to be captured. They have objectives. Services will be judged by utility to an agent, not ability to attract eyeballs.

A web built for agents is a web where structured data beats prose. Where cryptographic proof beats claims. Where efficiency beats engagement.

veda.ng/ai-agent-economy

The hard part: getting there without blowing up. 70% of big-bang AI deployments fail. The sequencing matters: veda.ng/stepwise-ai

How do you govern something smarter than you?

Every system of governance assumes Homo sapiens is the most intelligent actor. That assumption has held for 10,000 years. AGI may end it.

An AGI operating in financial markets could execute trades on a microsecond timescale. Human oversight becomes impossible. You need AI systems to monitor other AI systems. An arms race of control and counter-control.

The economic implications are equally profound. An AGI could automate not just manual labor but cognitive tasks: legal analysis, medical diagnosis, scientific research. How do you govern an economy where most human labor is obsolete?

Those who own the AGIs wield unprecedented power. An AGI-powered state could monitor every citizen, predict their thoughts, and preempt dissent. Not punished. Preempted.

We are preparing to govern gods. And we have very little time to figure out how.

veda.ng/agi-governance

Some governments aren't waiting. Estonia, Singapore, and the UAE already run AI at production scale across public services: veda.ng/agentic-state

The entry ramp is narrowing.

Stanford payroll data covering millions of American workers: early-career workers aged 22-25 in AI-exposed occupations experienced a 16% relative decline in employment since late 2022. Senior workers in the same fields? Stable or growing.

This held even after excluding the tech sector and remote jobs. A February 2026 update showed the gap widening with no sign of reversal.

Salesforce eliminated 4,000 customer support positions citing AI. Duolingo stopped using human contractors. Anthropic's CEO said nearly half of entry-level white-collar jobs could be replaced.

The question that matters most: whether economic gains from AI flow broadly or concentrate narrowly. Every previous technology eventually created broad prosperity. But the "eventually" ranged from one generation to three.

The difference with AI: it affects cognitive work. The communities affected are not regional towns. They are entry-level professionals in every major city.

veda.ng/ai-economy

The companies that get AI deployment right start small. One boring task. Three-month payback. Then scale: veda.ng/stepwise-ai

The best interface is no interface.

We went from desktop to pocket to wrist. Each step made interaction more immediate and less obtrusive. But all still require a conscious act. A tap, a word, a gesture.

Ambient intelligence is the final step: eliminate the command itself.

A kitchen that knows you returned from a run and suggests a hydrating smoothie. A meeting room that recognizes participants, pulls up project files, and starts transcribing when everyone sits down.

The design goal is not to create an engaging screen. It is to make the interface disappear entirely. The best ambient system is one you do not notice.

The smartphone era hijacks attention with notifications. Ambient intelligence filters the noise. Only what truly requires your input reaches you.

Privacy is the obvious challenge. A world that senses constantly is a world that collects constantly. Trust requires local processing, transparent control, and strong encryption.

veda.ng/ambient-intelligence

Before no interface, there's a simpler one. The text field is replacing the dashboard: veda.ng/universal-text-ui

The nation-state as a platform.

Instead of navigating government websites and offices to register a business, you make a single authenticated API call. Instead of convoluted tax filing, your software interfaces directly with the tax authority in real time.

Governance as a service. Public services become a library of functions. The state becomes a foundational layer, like AWS for civic life.

Citizenship becomes a set of cryptographic credentials in a digital wallet. To prove your age, you reveal only that you are over 18. No date of birth. No other data. Self-sovereign identity.

States would compete on service quality. A state with an inefficient business registration API loses customers to one with a better offering. A global marketplace for governance.

Estonia already built this. Their e-Residency program lets anyone become a digital resident and access their services from anywhere.

veda.ng/api-states

Estonia already built this. 99% of services digital. Babies born get benefits automatically. No forms: veda.ng/agentic-state

Logic finds answers. Intuition finds questions.

Human cognition operates on two levels. Slow, deliberate reasoning. And fast, associative intuition. The gut feeling. The expert's instant recognition.

AI has focused almost exclusively on replicating the first mode. Systems that excel at logic and math but struggle with ambiguity and creative problem-solving.

The next frontier: giving machines synthetic intuition. Not consciousness. But the ability to process information holistically, recognizing promising paths based on deeply encoded patterns.

Imagine knowledge as a vast network. Concepts are nodes. Relationships are weighted edges. A standard algorithm traverses paths methodically. An intuitive system assesses the entire topology, finding novel connections between distant concepts.

A purely rational approach to business innovation analyzes market data for incremental improvements. An intuitive approach connects unrelated trends with unmet needs. Breakthroughs.

veda.ng/artificial-intuition

When does superintelligence arrive? The honest answer: nobody knows.

Metaculus estimates 50% chance of AGI by 2040-2050. But the distribution is huge. Some predict 2030. Some predict 2100.

Moore's Law is slowing. Transistors can only get so small. But progress now comes from architecture, not hardware. Better algorithms. Better training methods.

We are running out of high-quality human data. Solutions exist: synthetic data, video modalities, reinforcement learning. Each has tradeoffs but none is a dead end.

The biggest jumps came from surprises. Transformers in 2017. Scaling laws in 2020. Nobody predicted them exactly.

If the scaling hypothesis is true, superintelligence is inevitable. If false, we are missing something fundamental. Everything hinges on which is true.

The uncertainty is not a reason for complacency. It is a reason for urgency.

veda.ng/asi-timeline

Klarna automated customer service. Saved $60M. Then customer satisfaction collapsed and they hired people back.

The failure wasn't AI. It was sequence. They skipped straight to the hardest problem — nuanced human interactions — instead of starting where machines already win: invoice matching, log parsing, email routing.

70% of companies that try to automate everything at once fail. Companies that pick one boring, high-frequency task first? Payback in under three months.

Discipline beats ambition every time.

veda.ng/stepwise-ai

Every dropdown on a dashboard is a question someone predicted you'd ask. Your actual question is almost never one of them.

PostHog replaced their analytics dashboard with a text field. Salesforce did it for CRM — 84% of queries now self-resolve. Cursor did it for code and hit $1B ARR.

The pattern: a text field backed by an LLM that calls APIs directly. No menus. No navigation. Just ask.

The dashboard era is ending.

veda.ng/universal-text-ui

In Estonia, only two government services require you to show up in person: marriages and divorces. Everything else is digital. 99% of public services.

Singapore runs AI across 100,000 public officers. UAE hit 97% AI utilization in government.

These aren't pilots. They're production systems serving millions.

A baby is born. The system detects it, calculates benefits, deposits money. No form. No queue. No portal. The citizen does nothing.

Most governments still make you download a PDF.

veda.ng/agentic-state

Lead response time: 4 hours. Contact rates drop 10x after 5 minutes.

One voice agent. One task: qualify and book. Response time fell to 60 seconds. Conversions tripled. 2,000+ meetings per month, no humans involved.

The sales team stopped qualifying. They only close now.

veda.ng/stepwise-ai

Estonia lets anyone on earth start an EU company remotely. 135,000 people from 170 countries have.

€125M in state revenue last year. 12:1 ROI. 5,556 new companies from non-citizens.

Government as infrastructure. Citizenship as credential. Geography as optional.

veda.ng/agentic-state

106 SaaS apps per company. 1,200 daily app switches per employee. 39% of time in deep focus.

93% of business leaders say they'd perform better asking data questions in plain English.

That's not a preference. It's a structural bottleneck.

veda.ng/universal-text-ui

Three-person agency. Six hours per person every Friday compiling client reports from 10+ data sources.

Routed analytics into an LLM. Twenty hours freed weekly. Client load: 30 → 50 campaigns. Zero hires.

AI didn't replace anyone. It removed a bottleneck.

veda.ng/stepwise-ai

Singapore's government AI chatbot: 50M citizen queries handled. AI call transcription: 72% less after-call work.

100,000 officers using Pair, saving 50 min per task.

2026 budget: "scaled deployment at national speed."

Most countries are still debating whether to start a pilot.

veda.ng/agentic-state

Estonia's X-Road: 1 billion+ government queries per year. Birth registered → population registry, health insurance, social services all updated automatically. One notification. Zero forms.

Operational since 2001. 25 years in production. Not a pilot.

Most countries digitized paper forms. Estonia redesigned the process.

veda.ng/agentic-state

CLI → GUI → NLI. Three paradigms. Each cut adaptation cost by 10x.

The GUI didn't improve the command line. It replaced it. Natural language won't improve dashboards. Same thing.

veda.ng/universal-text-ui

Gartner: 40% of agentic AI projects risk cancellation by 2027. Only 33% meet ROI targets. Only 21% confident in governance.

The tech works. The organizational readiness doesn't.

Fix: one task that pays for itself in 90 days. Then expand.

veda.ng/stepwise-ai

Abu Dhabi: AED 13B to become first AI-native government by 2027. 97% AI utilization. 108 HR services automated. One university cut faculty workload 95%.

No legacy systems. No tech debt. Clean-sheet AI government.
